hints.text: Plates long white + EU strip (Italy: 2 strips & shorter; Switzerland: no strip)
White bollards w/ black top, **dark-red or dark-grey reflector**, often a nipple-shaped black cap (sometimes snow pole on top)

shared_with.diff: Bright red → Slovenia; dark red/grey → Austria.

shared_with.note: Same design w/ bright red reflector.
Germany = simple white bollards w/ grey reflectors, no black top.

hints.text: Mostly mountainous + forested; east/northeast flatter & agricultural
**Alpine architecture**: light walls, wooden balconies/top-floor panelling, dark overhanging wooden roofs (S-Germany similar)
**German** (Ä Ö Ü ß); camera meta separates the German trio: Austria = Gen3 **and** Gen4; Germany = only Gen4; Switzerland = always low-cam

shared_with.diff: **Gen3 coverage → Austria** (Germany has none); Einbahnstraße vs EINBAHN.

hints.text: Direction + town-entry signs: **white w/ blue outline + unique font** (Germany: yellow town signs); green facility-direction signs common
Pedestrian sign: **two horizontal dashed lines** instead of zebra stripes (all neighbours use stripes)
hints.uniq_note: Belgium and Poland also use line-based designs — but within the Alpine neighbourhood this sign means Austria.

shared_with.note: Belgian pedestrian signs use two dotted lines PLUS a square-headed walking figure.
shared_with.diff: Plain lines, no figure → Austria; added figure → Belgium.

shared_with.note: Polish pedestrian signs use a SINGLE horizontal line (Austria uses two).
shared_with.diff: Two lines → Austria; one line → Poland.

hints.text: Signs sometimes mounted with **4 metal clips** (unique in Central Europe); road markers **blue w/ white numbers + one decimal comma** (SI: white, DE: white outline); **EINBAHN** one-way signs (DE: Einbahnstraße); 'Anfang'/'Ende' on no-stopping signs; white upside-down-T dead-end signs
Bus stops: **yellow-green H circle** (only Germany similar)

shared_with.diff: Blue-outline town signs → Austria; yellow town signs → Germany.

hints.text: **Yellow natural-gas poles** (snow-pole lookalikes) — unique to Austria
Gen4 smallcam coverage (like most of Western Europe)

**Elevation = region**: central+west most mountainous; 'flat' east still rolling; tallest sharpest peaks → the panhandle on the Italian border; **vineyards only in the far east** (Vienna + Lake Neusiedl)
**A- and B-road numbers increase east → west** (exception: B222–230 = Vienna); state flags + per-state **bus companies** (logo on the bus)

**State metas**: Vorarlberg = neon-yellow bus-stop poles + yellow-white pole stripes + white km markers (also one W-Salzburg road); Salzburg = white painted square next to km markers; Burgenland = green 'Güterweg' signs; Upper Austria = white Güterweg w/ black outline; Lower Austria = **yellow-blue sign borders** (state flag)
**Tin-foil antenna** → western Austria near the Swiss border (Austria-unique)

**Vienna**: dark-blue plaques w/ white serif font — **leading number = district**; Salzburg = unique font; Klagenfurt = irregular white border; St. Pölten = rounded white border; Vienna+Innsbruck poles w/ **red-white flag tape** (Vienna: thinner, usually two per pole)
**Lake Constance** on the western border — vast, unlike any mountain lake; Gen2 only on one Burgenland road + ski trekkers
